Identify the initial problem, constraints, hypotheses, design iterations, experimental validations, failures/partial results, revisions, and final outcomes.

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper addresses the need for a DNA assembly method that is iterative, seamless, and capable of assembling repetitive sequences, to facilitate DBTL cycles in metabolic engineering for threonine and 1-propanol production. Existing methods like BioBrick (Type IIP REs) leave scars and are not seamless, while Golden Gate (Type IIS REs) lacks reusability. PS-Brick combines Type IIP and IIS REs to achieve iterative, seamless, and repetitive sequence assembly.

section_gists.heading: A seamless and iterative DNA assembly method named PS-Brick and its assisted metabolic engineering for threonine and 1-propanol production
section_gists.gist: Title page with authors and affiliations.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: PS-Brick is a Type IIP and IIS RE-assisted method for iterative, seamless, and repetitive sequence assembly. One round takes hours, with high efficiency (10^4-10^5 CFU/μg DNA) and accuracy (~90%). Applied to threonine production via DBTL cycles, achieving 45.71 g/L threonine. Also used for CRISPR array construction and 1-propanol pathway assembly (1.35 g/L).
process_signals: Method development driven by need for iterative, scarless, and repetitive sequence assembly in metabolic engineering.
Application to threonine production involved stepwise DBTL cycles: feedback regulation release, bottleneck elimination, export intensification, catabolism inactivation.
Seamless cloning enabled codon saturation mutagenesis and bicistronic design.
Repetitive sequence cloning enabled tandem CRISPR sgRNA arrays.
Heterologous 1-propanol pathway assembled in one cycle.
methods: PS-Brick: combines Type IIP and IIS REs, uses PCR fragments and purified plasmids.
Applied to E. coli for threonine and 1-propanol production.

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: Metabolic engineering requires iterative DBTL cycles, needing iterative DNA assembly methods. Existing RE-based methods: BioBrick (Type IIP) leaves scars, not seamless; Golden Gate (Type IIS) limited reusability. MoClo/Golden Braid enable iterative but complex. PS-Brick aims to combine strengths of both RE types for iterative, seamless, and repetitive assembly.
process_signals: Motivation: limitations of current methods—BioBrick scars (8 nt) hinder protein fusions; Golden Gate not reusable; MoClo/Golden Braid complex.
Gap: no method combines iterative, seamless, and repetitive assembly simply.
Hypothesis: using both Type IIP and IIS REs can achieve these properties.

section_gists.heading: Design and proof-of-concept of PS-Brick assembly scheme The design principles of PS-Brick
section_gists.gist: PS-Brick uses Type IIP (SphI) and Type IIS (BmrI for TA cloning, MlyI for blunt end ligation) REs. Vectors pOB and pOM created with entrance sites. PCR products digested with SphI, ligated to vector. Assembly efficiency 4-8×10^4 CFU/μg DNA, accuracy 88.2-95.1%. Seamless joints confirmed. Workflow completed in half a day. Comparison with traditional cloning showed similar efficiency. Blunt end ligation allows complete seamless assembly; TA cloning offers more RE options. Self-ligation of undigested vector reduced accuracy; dephosphorylation suggested.
process_signals: Design hypothesis: combine Type IIP and IIS REs to achieve iterative and seamless assembly.
Proof-of-concept: constructed vectors pOB (BmrI/SphI) and pOM (MlyI/SphI).
Tested cutting efficiency: BmrI and MlyI cut efficiently in 15 min, but uncut plasmid background required longer digestion (30 min) to reduce transformation.
SphI digestion time (15-180 min) did not affect assembly efficiency or accuracy significantly.
Accuracy ranged 88.2-95.1%; failures due to self-ligation of vector not fully digested by SphI.
Blunt end/MlyI-based PS-Brick is completely sequence-independent, preferred for universal application.
TA clone-based PS-Brick has more RE options (BmrI, BciVI, HphI).
Optimization needed: dephosphorylation to prevent self-ligation.
methods: PS-Brick assembly: vector pOB/pOM double-digested with SphI and BmrI/MlyI; PCR products digested with SphI; ligation.
TA cloning (BmrI) and blunt end ligation (MlyI) variants.
Transformation into E. coli DH5α.
Assembly efficiency: CFU/μg DNA.
Accuracy: sequencing of 20 clones.
metrics: Cutting time: 15 min sufficient for REs
Workflow time: half a day for one round
failures_or_limits: Uncut plasmid background reduced transformation efficiency; resolved by longer digestion (30 min).
Self-ligation of vector due to incomplete SphI digestion; suggested dephosphorylation.
Blunt end ligation expected less efficient but similar due to enzyme choice.

section_gists.heading: Optimization of the PS-Brick
section_gists.gist: Cutting efficiency of BmrI and MlyI tested: 15 min sufficient, but transformation test showed uncut plasmids reduced over time; 30 min used. SphI digestion time (15-180 min) gave similar assembly efficiency (~4-8×10^4 CFU/μg DNA) and accuracy (88.2-95.1%). Workflow time: half a day. Comparison with traditional SphI/XbaI cloning showed similar efficiency. Blunt end ligation (MlyI) allows complete seamless assembly; TA cloning (BmrI) has more RE options. Self-ligation of vector identified as cause of incorrect clones; dephosphorylation recommended.
process_signals: Optimization: tested cutting times for BmrI and MlyI; 15 min enough but 30 min used to reduce uncut plasmid.
SphI digestion time did not affect efficiency/accuracy.
Self-ligation issue: clones without insert had original vector sequence, indicating incomplete SphI digestion.
Solution: dephosphorylation to prevent self-ligation.
Blunt end/MlyI-based PS-Brick is completely seamless and sequence-independent.
TA clone-based PS-Brick offers more RE choices.
methods: Gel electrophoresis and transformation tests for cutting efficiency.
Assembly efficiency and accuracy measured as before.
Comparison with traditional cloning using SphI/XbaI.
metrics: Workflow: half a day per round
failures_or_limits: Uncut plasmid background: resolved by 30 min digestion.
Blunt end ligation efficiency similar to cohesive end due to enzyme choice.

section_gists.heading: DBTL cycles for pathway engineering for the production of threonine and its derivate 1-propanol through PS-Brick
section_gists.gist: PS-Brick applied to threonine overproduction via iterative DBTL cycles: release feedback regulation, intensify biosynthesis, eliminate rate-limiting steps, inactivate catabolism, modify transport, reinforce coenzyme regeneration. Four rounds of DBTL cycle performed stepwise through six cycles of PS-Brick reactions, gradually increasing threonine production.
process_signals: Application strategy: stepwise DBTL cycles using PS-Brick.
General metabolic engineering strategy for threonine outlined.
Four DBTL rounds performed, each resolving one aspect.
methods: PS-Brick used for sequential assembly of genetic modifications.
DBTL cycles: design, build, test, learn.

section_gists.heading: Site-directed mutagenesis for feedback-resistant ThrA
section_gists.gist: Seamless PS-Brick used for codon saturation mutagenesis of ThrA residue 433 to relieve feedback inhibition. 20 mutants generated in one cycle. ThrA-Gly433Phe gave 48-fold higher threonine than wild-type. Vector pOthr created with HindIII/MlyI entrance sites after removing internal MlyI sites. Seamless in-frame fusion enabled precise mutagenesis.
process_signals: First DBTL cycle: release feedback regulation.
Design: saturation mutagenesis at Gly433 of ThrA using seamless PS-Brick.
Vector preparation: removed four MlyI sites from pACYC184 to create pOthr.
20 PCR donors with different codons assembled in one cycle.
Result: ThrA-Gly433Phe mutant best, 48-fold increase.
Seamless assembly crucial for in-frame fusion.
methods: PS-Brick with HindIII (Type IIP) and MlyI (Type IIS).
Codon saturation mutagenesis via primer design.
Shake flask fermentation for threonine measurement.
metrics: Threonine titer: 48-fold increase with Gly433Phe mutant
failures_or_limits: Internal MlyI sites in pACYC184 had to be removed before use.

section_gists.heading: Identification of the metabolic bottleneck for threonine synthesis
section_gists.gist: Second DBTL cycle: identify bottleneck after thrABC overexpression. Five candidate genes (ppc, aspA, aspC, asd, pntAB) assembled into previous vector via PS-Brick. Only asd overexpression increased threonine (56.7%). Others decreased production (e.g., ppc -60.8%). asd identified as bottleneck; negative effects of overexpressing non-rate-limiting steps noted.
process_signals: Hypothesis: unknown bottleneck after thrABC overexpression.
Tested five genes in parallel using PS-Brick.
Result: asd is bottleneck; others detrimental.
Learning: overexpression of non-rate-limiting steps can decrease production due to futile cycles.
Iterative PS-Brick allowed sequential addition of genes.
methods: PS-Brick assembly of ppc, aspA, aspC, asd, pntAB into pACYC184-thrA^433 BC.
Transcriptional analysis by RT-PCR.
Shake flask fermentation.
failures_or_limits: Overexpression of ppc, aspC, aspA, pntAB decreased threonine, indicating they are not bottlenecks and cause futile cycles.

section_gists.heading: Prioritization of isoenzymes for threonine efflux
section_gists.gist: Third DBTL cycle: prioritize threonine exporters. Four exporters (rhtA, rhtB, rhtC, yeaS) assembled with same promoter (P_T) and RBS (BCD1) via two PS-Brick cycles. Seamless assembly enabled precise BCD1-exporter fusion. rhtC overexpression gave highest threonine (3.7 g/L from 0.9 g/L).

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: PS-Brick is first method combining Type IIP and IIS REs for iterative, seamless, repetitive assembly. Addresses limitations of BioBrick (scars), Golden Gate (reusability), MoClo/Golden Braid (complexity). Advantages: high efficiency/accuracy, low cost, speed, simplicity. Demonstrated in threonine DBTL cycles and 1-propanol pathway. Threonine titer 45.71 g/L; 1-propanol titer low (1.35 g/L) compared to 10.8 g/L in literature. Future work: biosensors, chromosomal integration, combinatorial strategies.

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: PS-Brick is the first RE-assisted method using both Type IIP and IIS REs, combining iterative and seamless strengths. It is efficient, simple, and addresses three desirable features. Valuable addition to synthetic biology and metabolic engineering toolbox.
process_signals: Summary of achievement: PS-Brick successfully developed and applied.
Key features: iterative, seamless, repetitive assembly.
Positioning: first method to combine both RE types.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The initial problem was the lack of a simple DNA assembly method that is simultaneously iterative, seamless, and capable of handling repetitive sequences, which are all needed for efficient DBTL cycles in metabolic engineering.

The hypothesis was that combining Type IIP REs (for iterative assembly via reusable sites) and Type IIS REs (for seamless assembly via custom overhangs) could achieve all three properties in a single method.

The development followed a design-build-test cycle: design vectors with entrance sites, test cutting/ligation efficiency, optimize to reduce background (uncut plasmid, self-ligation), and validate with multiple use cases.

The application to threonine production was structured as sequential DBTL cycles, each addressing a specific metabolic engineering target (feedback resistance, bottleneck, export, catabolism), demonstrating the iterative power of PS-Brick.
The method was extended to challenging scenarios: repetitive CRISPR arrays and heterologous pathway assembly, showing versatility.
Limitations encountered (internal RE sites, self-ligation, low 1-propanol titer) were acknowledged and partially addressed, with suggestions for future improvements.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Whether the triple CRISPR mutant could be obtained with further optimization.
The exact efficiency improvement after dephosphorylation (not quantified in text).
Whether PS-Brick can be automated or scaled for high-throughput assembly.
The performance of PS-Brick with very large constructs or multiple fragments.
The generalizability to other organisms beyond E. coli.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Lack of a simple DNA assembly method that is simultaneously iterative, seamless, and capable of handling repetitive sequences for efficient DBTL cycles in metabolic engineering.

canonical_events.summary: Current DNA assembly methods (BioBrick, Golden Gate, MoClo/Golden Braid) lack a simple technique that is simultaneously iterative, seamless, and capable of handling repetitive sequences, needed for efficient DBTL cycles in metabolic engineering.
Combining Type IIP and IIS restriction endonucleases in a single assembly method (PS-Brick) can achieve iterative, seamless, and repetitive sequence assembly.

PS-Brick design uses Type IIS REs BmrI (1 nt overhang) and MlyI (blunt end) combined with Type IIP RE SphI to create reusable entrance sites for iterative assembly. Vectors pOB and pOM constructed from pUC19.

Optimization of PS-Brick: cutting efficiency of BmrI and MlyI tested; 30 min digestion chosen to reduce uncut plasmid background. SphI digestion time (15-180 min) did not affect assembly efficiency (~4-8×10^4 CFU/μg DNA) or accuracy (88.2-95.1%).
Self-ligation of vector due to incomplete SphI digestion identified as cause of incorrect clones; dephosphorylation suggested to prevent self-ligation.

Comparison of PS-Brick with traditional Type IIP RE-based cloning (XbaI/SphI) showed similar efficiency (~65,000-70,000 CFU/μg) and accuracy (85-90%). Blunt end/MlyI-based PS-Brick is completely seamless and sequence-independent; TA clone/BmrI-based PS-Brick offers more RE options.

First DBTL cycle: Seamless PS-Brick used for codon saturation mutagenesis of ThrA residue 433 to relieve feedback inhibition. 20 mutants generated in one cycle; ThrA-Gly433Phe gave 48-fold higher threonine than wild-type.

Second DBTL cycle: Five candidate genes (ppc, aspA, aspC, asd, pntAB) tested for bottleneck after thrABC overexpression. Only asd overexpression increased threonine (56.7%); others decreased production (e.g., ppc -60.8%). asd identified as bottleneck.

Third DBTL cycle: Four threonine exporters (rhtA, rhtB, rhtC, yeaS) prioritized using bicistronic design (BCD1) and mid-strength promoter. rhtC overexpression gave highest threonine (3.7 g/L from 0.9 g/L).

Fourth DBTL cycle: Repetitive sgRNA arrays for tdh and ilvA deletion assembled via TA clone/BciVI PS-Brick. Double mutant strain obtained; fed-batch produced 45.71 g/L threonine, 28.7% higher than control.

Heterologous 1-propanol pathway (kivD and ADH2) assembled in one PS-Brick cycle. Fed-batch produced 1.35 g/L 1-propanol, but precursors accumulated (threonine 21.01 g/L, 2-ketobutyrate 0.89 g/L), indicating low enzyme activity.
Low 1-propanol titer attributed to insufficient activity of heterologous KivD and ADH2 enzymes; future work should focus on obtaining more efficient enzymes.

PS-Brick is unfit for combinatorial pathway engineering due to limitations in multi-part assembly, and the need to eliminate internal restriction sites remains a general limitation for RE-based strategies.

PS-Brick successfully demonstrated for iterative DBTL cycles in threonine overproduction, achieving 45.71 g/L threonine by deleting two genes and overexpressing two genes and one operon. PS-Brick is the first restriction-ligation assembly method using both Type IIP and IIS REs, combining iterative and seamless strengths.

initial_request.known_context: Current methods (BioBrick, Golden Gate, MoClo/Golden Braid) each lack one or more of the desired properties: iterative, seamless, repetitive sequence assembly.
quantifiable_goals: Achieve high assembly efficiency (10^4-10^5 CFU/μg DNA)

evidence.quote_or_span: An application of metabolic engineering to threonine production, including the release of feedback regulation, elimination of metabolic bottlenecks, intensification of threonine export and inactivation of threonine catabolism, was stepwise resolved in E. coli by rounds of “design-build-test-learn” cycles through the iterative PS-Brick paradigm, and 45.71 g/L threonine was obtained through fed-batch fermentation.

seamless cloning enabled precise in-frame fusion for codon saturation mutagenesis and bicistronic design, and the repetitive sequence cloning ability of PS-Brick enabled construction of tandem CRISPR sgRNA arrays for genome editing.
the PS-Brick framework is the first restriction-ligation assembly method using both Type IIP and IIS REs
we have developed a novel PS-Brick framework that enables iterative, seamless and sequence-repeat DNA assembly.

success_verification.final_conclusion: PS-Brick is the first restriction-ligation assembly method using both Type IIP and IIS REs, enabling iterative, seamless, and repetitive sequence assembly. It was successfully applied to threonine overproduction (45.71 g/L) through four DBTL cycles and to 1-propanol pathway assembly (1.35 g/L), though low enzyme activity limited 1-propanol yield.
limitations: Low 1-propanol titer due to insufficient KivD and ADH2 activity
PS-Brick unfit for combinatorial pathway engineering due to limitations in multi-part assembly
Need to eliminate internal restriction sites remains a general limitation for RE-based strategies
